name: ESP8266 NodeMCU
fullName: ESP8266 NodeMCU v1.0 Wi-Fi Microcontroller Board
tagline: Wi-Fi enabled microcontroller board for IoT projects — programs just like Arduino
description: The ESP8266 NodeMCU is a low-cost Wi-Fi microcontroller board built around the ESP8266 chip by Espressif. It can be programmed using the Arduino IDE, supports station and access point Wi-Fi modes, and is widely used for IoT projects like home automation, weather stations, data logging to the cloud, and web-controlled devices. It's smaller and more powerful than Arduino UNO with built-in Wi-Fi.

specs.operatingVoltage: 3.3V (5V tolerant via onboard regulator on NodeMCU)
specs.gpioCount: 11 usable GPIO pins (some with PWM, I2C, SPI, UART)

pinout.name: D0
pinout.desc: GPIO, no PWM, no I2C, can wake from deep sleep

pinout.name: D3
pinout.desc: GPIO, pulled HIGH — LOW at boot enters flash mode

pinout.name: D4
pinout.desc: GPIO, built-in LED — pulled HIGH at boot

pinout.name: D8
pinout.desc: GPIO, pulled LOW — HIGH at boot causes boot failure

pinout.name: Analog In
pinout.desc: 10-bit ADC, max 3.3V input (1V on bare ESP8266 chip)

pinout.name: VIN
pinout.desc: 5V input via USB or external supply

wiringNote: All GPIOs are 3.3V logic — do NOT connect 5V signals directly. Use a voltage divider or level shifter. D3, D4, D8 have boot-mode restrictions — avoid connecting things that drive them LOW/HIGH at power-on.
libraryInstall: Arduino IDE → File → Preferences → Additional Boards URL: http://arduino.esp8266.com/stable/package_esp8266com_index.json → Board Manager → Install 'esp8266'
commonMistakes: Connecting 5V logic signals directly → damages 3.3V GPIO pins
Drawing too much current from 3V3 pin → NodeMCU regulator overheats (max ~300mA)
Not installing CH340G/CP2102 driver → board not recognized by PC
Using D3, D4, D8 as outputs during boot → causes boot failure or enter flash mode
